topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the apartment had become a heavy blanket, suffocating Leo. He stared at the open laptop screen, the words swimming before his eyes—a meticulously detailed description of his morning routine, the way he habitually chewed his pen, the faint scar above his left eyebrow. A cold knot tightened in his stomach. He’d found the manuscript tucked beneath a stack of grocery receipts, a ghost of a secret he hadn't known existed. His breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air. He felt utterly hollowed out, the energy draining from his limbs as if someone had siphoned it clean. When Sarah finally walked in, humming a cheerful tune, Leo didn't look up. He just nodded, a jerky, minimal movement, his gaze fixed on the floor, the weight of the revelation pressing him into the worn cushions of the sofa.

[story 2]

"It's just... another thing," Mark mumbled, his voice sandpaper dry. He traced the outline of the title on the screen with a hesitant finger, the letters seeming to mock him. Every sentence was a mirror reflecting a version of himself he barely recognized, polished and framed by someone else's prose. A profound weariness settled over him, a leaden feeling in his chest that made rising from the chair feel like scaling a sheer cliff face. He pushed his chair back with a soft scrape that echoed too loudly in the quiet room. When Clara finally caught his eye, her expression of concern felt like a physical blow. He managed a weak smile, a performance he couldn't sustain, his shoulders slumping forward in a gesture of utter defeat.

[story 3]

Elara found the document tucked inside a seldom-used journal, the ink flowing with an intimate, almost painful detail. Reading it felt like wading through thick mud; the narrative was so intensely focused on her small, unguarded moments that it left her feeling exposed and strangely diminished. Her hands began to tremble, a fine vibration running up her arms. She sank onto the floor, the cool wood pressing against her knees. The world seemed muted, the colors in the room leaching away. When Ben approached, offering a tentative hand, Elara flinched almost imperceptibly. Her voice, when it finally emerged, was thin and reedy, barely above a whisper, conveying a deep, internal exhaustion that spoke volumes about the burden she was suddenly carrying.
